quality.rationale: 多步相对论运动学与热力学结合，含洛伦兹不变量推导、角度变换与立体角积分，物理背景清晰

origin.question: Problem 5.13. A thermally-conducting black sphere with a thermometer attached moves with velocity $\mathbf{v}$ through a black body radiation field of temperature $\mathbf{T}_0$ . What does the thermometer read?
origin.solution:
Solution 5.13. An observer on the sphere sees the black body spectrum doppler shifted by different amounts in different directions. Since the black body spectrum has $\mathrm{I}_{
u} / 
u^{3} = 	ext{constant} 	imes (\mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{h}
u / \mathrm{KT}} - 1)^{-1}$ , and since $\mathrm{I}_{
u} / 
u^{3}$ is a Lorentz invariant, the effect of a doppler shift $
u_{0}ightarrow 
u$ is just to change the effective temperature $\mathrm{T}_{0}ightarrow \mathrm{T} = \mathrm{T}_{0}(
u / 
u_{0})$ .
What is the doppler shift seen by the comoving observer when he looks at an angle $	heta$ to the forward direction of motion? This standard result is easy to rederive with invariants: Let $\mathbf{u}$ be the 4-velocity of the sphere, $\mathbf{u}_0$ be the rest 4-velocity of the radiation, and $\mathbf{p}$ be the 4-momentum of a photon in units of Planck's constant. Then $
u = -\mathbf{u} \cdot \mathbf{p}$ , $
u_0 = -\mathbf{u}_0 \cdot \mathbf{p}$ and $(1 - v^2)^{- rac{1}{2}} \equiv \gamma = -\mathbf{u} \cdot \mathbf{u}_0$ . In the sphere's frame,
$$
\mathbf {u} = (1, \underline {{0}}), \quad \mathbf {u}_{0} = (\gamma , - \gamma \underline {{v}}), \quad \mathbf {p} = (
u , 
u \underline {{n}})
$$
where $\underline{\mathbf{n}}$ is a unit 3-vector. The unit spatial vector in the direction of $\underline{\mathbf{v}}$ is
$$
\left(0,  rac {\underline {{v}}}{| v |}ight) = -  rac {u_{0} + (u_{0} \cdot u) u}{\gamma v}.
$$
(This is verified by substituting in components in the sphere's frame.) Similarly, the vector $(0,\underline{n})$ is invariantly written as
$$
(0, \underline {{\mathbf {n}}}) =  rac {\mathbf {p} + (\mathbf {u} \cdot \mathbf {p}) \mathbf {u}}{
u},
$$
so the angle of the photon in the sphere's frame is
$$
\cos 	heta = (0, \underline {{n}}) \cdot \left(0,  rac {\underline {{v}}}{| v |}ight) =  rac {
u_{0} - \gamma 
u}{\gamma v 
u}.
$$
The last line is obtained by dotting together the invariant 4-vector expressions and using $
u = -\mathbf{u} \cdot \mathbf{p}$ etc. Solving for $
u / 
u_0$ , we have
$$
 rac {\mathbf {T}}{\mathbf {T}_{0}} =  rac {
u}{
u_{0}} =  rac {\mathbf {1}}{\gamma (\mathbf {1} + \mathbf {v} \cos 	heta)}.
$$
Finally, the equilibrium temperature is given by the average over all directions $< \mathrm{T}(	heta)^{4} >^{ rac{1}{4}}$ . [This is because the radiation rate, which equals the absorption rate in equilibrium, goes as $\mathrm{T}^{4}$ (Stefan-Boltzman law).]
Thus
$$
\left\langle  rac {T}{T_{0}}ightangle^{4} =  rac {1}{2} \int_{\cos 	heta = - 1}^{\cos 	heta = 1}  rac {d (\cos 	heta)}{\gamma^{4} (1 + v \cos 	heta)^{4}} =  rac {1}{\gamma^{4}}  rac {1}{6 v} \left[  rac {1}{(1 - v)^{3}} -  rac {1}{(1 + v)^{3}}ight]
$$
and
$$
\mathrm {T}_{	ext {e qu il ib ri um}} = < \mathrm {T}^{4} >^{ rac {1}{4}} = (\gamma^{2} (1 + \mathrm {v}^{2} / 3))^{ rac {1}{4}} \mathrm {T}_{0}.
$$
